attributes.stem:
A 14-year-old presents to the General Practice clinic with their parent expressing feelings of depression. Their parent describes them always being a 'shy' child, but states they reported low mood and anxiety for the past four years, and that this has worsened over the past eight months. They have been taking sertraline for depression for the past year though report minimal effect. They report feeling uncomfortable in their body, and have particularly struggled with the onset of menstruation eight months ago, also reporting distress with recent breast development. They avoid looking in mirrors and wear loose-fitting clothes, though do not restrict their eating or engage in purging behaviour. They have difficulty participating in physical education classes and avoid using public bathrooms due to worrying about being seen by classmates. They deny any suicidal ideation or self-harm. Their parent notes they have become increasingly withdrawn and their school grades have declined.

On examination, the patient appears withdrawn but cooperative. Their vital signs are within normal limits. Mental state examination reveals a depressed mood with appropriate affect. There are no signs of psychosis or thought disorder. There is no evidence of self-harm.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Referral to a specialised eating disorder unit
Recommend using a contraceptive pill to skip periods
Referral to a gender identity service
attributes.explanation:
This teenager presents with features suggestive of gender dysphoria - a marked incongruence between their experienced gender and assigned gender, and strong desire to be rid of secondary sexual characteristics, accompanied by clinically significant distress. While they have a history of depression, the presentation suggests underlying gender identity concerns that require specialist assessment.

Referral to a gender identity service is the most appropriate next step. These services provide comprehensive assessment by mental health professionals experienced in gender diversity, who can evaluate the presence of gender dysphoria and develop an appropriate management plan. They can also provide support to both the young person and their family. These services will assess the young person's readiness and appropriateness for further treatment and can coordinate gender-affirming care as required.

Increase sertraline dose would not address the underlying gender identity concerns. While depression should continue to be managed, focusing solely on depression may miss the opportunity to address the core issues related to gender identity.

Referral to a specialised eating disorder unit would not be appropriate as there is no evidence of disordered eating behaviours. While the patient avoids mirrors and wears loose clothing, these are more likely related to gender dysphoria than body image concerns related to eating disorders.

Recommending a contraceptive pill to skip periods, while potentially providing some symptomatic relief, does not address the underlying gender identity concerns and should only be considered as part of a comprehensive management plan developed by specialists.

Prescribing a gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogue (puberty blocker) would be premature without specialist assessment. While these medications can be effective in preventing further development of secondary sexual characteristics, they require careful consideration of risks including effects on bone mineral density, fertility, and psychological development. Other gender-affirming medications like testosterone can have significant effects including voice deepening, increased muscle mass, and cessation of menstruation, but may also increase risks of cardiovascular disease, liver dysfunction, and mood changes. These treatments should only be initiated after comprehensive assessment and with informed consent.

The management of gender dysphoria requires a careful, staged approach with involvement of experienced clinicians. The focus should be on providing appropriate support while carefully assessing the young person's needs and wishes. Mental health support should continue throughout this process, recognising that gender dysphoria often co-exists with depression and anxiety, but may also be the primary cause of this patient's symptoms.
learningPoints: Comprehensive assessment by mental health professionals experienced in gender diversity is essential for diagnosis and treatment.
Gender-affirming medications can have significant side effects so informed consent prior to initiating therapy and careful monitoring is required.
references: Telfer MM, Tollit MA, Pace CC, Pang KC. Australian standards of care and treatment guidelines for transgender and gender diverse children and adolescents. Med J Aust. 2018 Aug 6;209(3):132-136. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29902964/

Tordoff DM, Wanta JW, Collin A, Stepney C, Inwards-Breland DJ, Ahrens K. Mental Health Outcomes in Transgender and Nonbinary Youths Receiving Gender-Affirming Care. JAMA Network Open [Internet]. 2022 Feb 25;5(2). Available at: https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2789423
American Psychiatric Association. Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. 5th ed. 2013.

attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
